does: This paper constructs a comprehensive multilingual benchmark suite containing seven datasets and over ten languages to evaluate how well current large language model guardrails detect and filter toxic content. The authors systematically test state-of-the-art guardrail systems, assess their vulnerability to recent jailbreaking techniques, and measure how in-context safety policies and language resource availability affect performance. Their evaluation reveals that existing guardrails perform poorly on multilingual toxicity detection and demonstrate low robustness against adversarial jailbreak prompts, highlighting critical gaps in current safety mechanisms across languages.
connects: This work extends prior research on LLM safety and toxicity detection by shifting focus from English-centric evaluation to truly multilingual settings. It builds on earlier toxicity benchmarks like those from the AI2 real toxicity prompts and related work on cross-lingual transfer learning, while also engaging with the rapidly growing body of research on jailbreaking attacks and LLM vulnerabilities. The paper responds to the broader concern in the safety community that safety mechanisms developed primarily for English may not generalize effectively to low-resource or typologically distant languages, addressing a gap highlighted in multilingual NLP research.
enables: This benchmark enables future development of more robust multilingual safety systems by identifying specific failure modes across languages and language families. It provides a foundation for researchers to design guardrails with better cross-lingual generalization and robustness against adversarial attacks. The work motivates investigation into whether guardrails need language-specific tuning, whether multilingual language models inherently struggle with toxicity in certain languages, and whether jailbreak techniques transfer across languages. It also points toward larger questions about resource allocation for safety in underrepresented languages and the development of evaluation frameworks that adequately cover global linguistic diversity in AI safety.
